chunk_summary: This section addresses co-management strategies for medication-associated weight gain in pediatric patients, emphasizing baseline documentation, metabolic monitoring, psychiatric coordination, lifestyle interventions, and adjunctive use of metformin as per guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of medication-associated weight gain in pediatric obesity and its management.
Learn the importance of baseline documentation and metabolic monitoring in weight-promoting therapies.
Explore co-management strategies with psychiatry to balance psychiatric stability and weight management.
Evaluate the role of metformin as an adjunct in managing antipsychotic-associated weight gain.
key_facts: Atypical antipsychotics, antidepressants, mood stabilizers, and glucocorticoids can cause rapid weight gain.
Baseline documentation should include weight, height, BMI category, BP, and waist circumference.
Metabolic monitoring should include glucose or HbA1c, lipids, and ALT, with schedules based on medication risk.
Metformin can modestly attenuate antipsychotic-associated weight gain in youth, as noted in the IAP guideline.
Metformin use requires slow titration and monitoring of renal function and long-term vitamin B12 status.
tags_mesh: Drug-Related Side Effects and Adverse Reactions
reference_quote: The IAP obesity guideline notes metformin as an adjunct option in selected settings, usually with specialist-linked monitoring.
document_key_topics: Medication-associated weight gain in pediatric obesity
Adjunctive use of metformin in weight management
